name: Tilottama
description: Tilottama, a prominent figure in Hindu mythology, is an Apsara, a celestial nymph celebrated for her unparalleled beauty and ethereal grace. Her name, meaning 'the finest sesame seed' or 'composed of the finest parts,' reflects her creation as an assemblage of the most exquisite qualities from across the universe. Created by the divine architect Vishwakarma under the direction of Brahma, Tilottama's primary purpose was to bring about the downfall of the arrogant and nearly invincible Asura brothers, Sunda and Upasunda. Their insatiable lust for her beauty led them to fight each other, ultimately resulting in their demise, thus restoring balance to the cosmos. Tilottama embodies the intoxicating power of beauty and the potential for it to both inspire and destroy. Her narratives often serve as cautionary tales about the dangers of unchecked desire and the cyclical nature of creation, preservation, and destruction within Hindu cosmology. She represents the divine feminine, shakti, in its most alluring and potentially disruptive form.
subtitle: Celestial Nymph of Exquisite Beauty

keyMyths.title: The Downfall of Sunda and Upasunda
keyMyths.description: Brahma, concerned by the growing power and arrogance of the Asura brothers Sunda and Upasunda, tasked Vishwakarma with creating the most beautiful woman in the universe. Vishwakarma crafted Tilottama from the finest elements of creation. When the brothers saw her, they became consumed by lust and fought each other for her possession, ultimately leading to their deaths. Tilottama thus fulfilled her purpose of restoring cosmic balance by sowing discord among the powerful demons.

keyMyths.title: Indra's Gaze and Gautama's Curse
keyMyths.description: In another narrative, Tilottama visits Indra, the king of gods. Indra, captivated by her beauty, develops a thousand eyes to gaze upon her from all directions. Another version states that Gautama cursed Indra with a thousand vaginas on his body, which were later transformed into eyes. This myth highlights the intoxicating and potentially dangerous nature of beauty and the challenges even gods face when confronted with overwhelming desire.

keyMyths.title: Curse by Shiva
keyMyths.description: Once, Tilottama circumambulated Shiva’s altar, and because of her beauty, each of Shiva's five faces gazed upon her. Parvati, Shiva's consort, became jealous. Shiva then cursed Tilottama to be born on Earth in a family of low caste. This myth illustrates the volatile nature of divine relationships and the potential consequences of perceived transgressions, even unintentional ones, within the celestial realm.

sources.author: Monier Monier-Williams
sources.description: Provides etymological analysis of the name Tilottama and its connection to beauty and creation.
sources.text: Provides etymological analysis of the name Tilottama and its connection to beauty and creation.

sources.author: Wendy Doniger
sources.work: Hindu Myths: A Sourcebook Translated from the Sanskrit
sources.description: Offers translations and interpretations of key myths featuring Tilottama, contextualizing her role within broader Hindu narratives.
